label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's read the passage carefully to figure out the correct answer. The passage talks about how memory can play tricks on us, sometimes leading us to remember things that didn't happen or forget things that did happen. The author gives two examples to illustrate this. First example: - The author got up one morning and found the front door was unlocked all night. - Yet, the author "clearly remember[ed] locking it carefully the night before." So, according to the passage, the author thought they locked the door (they clearly remember doing so), but in reality, they must have forgotten to lock it because the door was found unlocked. The memory trick is that they remembered locking the door, but it wasn't actually locked. Now let's look at the options: (A) One night the writer forgot to lock the front door. - This seems correct as the door was found unlocked, though the author thought they locked it. (B) One night the writer forgot having locked the front door. - This is a bit tricky. The author didn't forget having locked the door, they remembered locking it, just it didn't happen in reality. (C) The writer remembered to lock the door. - This is close to being correct, but the phrasing is awkward. The author "clearly remember[ed] locking it carefully," which means they remembered locking the door, but the door was actually not locked. (D) The writer remembered unlocking the front door. - This is incorrect because the author remembered locking the door, not unlocking it. So the correct answer is (A) - the writer forgot to lock the front door (the door was found unlocked) even though they remembered (falsely) having locked it. Let me re-read the passage to confirm. "I got up and found that I had left the front door unlocked all night, yet I clearly remember locking it carefully the night before." This confirms that the author didn't actually lock the door (it was left unlocked), but they remembered locking it. Therefore, they forgot to lock the door. Option A is correct. The other options are not supported by the passage: - (B) is incorrect because the author didn't forget having locked the door; they remembered it clearly. - (C) is incorrect because it's not just
